stories.headline: Denver mayor backs moratorium on new data center construction pending regulatory review
stories.summary:
Mayor Mike Johnston announced Monday that Denver is considering a temporary halt on new data center projects to allow city officials to review and potentially overhaul regulations governing the facilities. The pause, described as lasting "several months," would take effect only after a majority of the 13-member city council approves an ordinance. Once in place, it would not affect existing centers, those under construction or projects already permitted.

The proposed moratorium is aimed at developing data center-specific zoning focused on "responsible land, energy and water use" and affordability for utility ratepayers. City officials plan to consult residents, climate experts and industry leaders in crafting new rules. Council members Paul Kashmann and Darrell Watson expressed general support, though Kashmann noted remaining disagreements with the mayor's approach.

The announcement comes amid growing community concern about a CoreSite data center under construction in the Elyria-Swansea neighborhood of north Denver. That project, CoreSite's largest in Denver at more than 590,000 square feet, would not be immediately affected by the moratorium. Residents in the predominantly Latino Globeville, Elyria and Swansea neighborhoods have organized to demand the company address impacts on electricity bills, water use and generator emissions.

The move also coincides with debates at the state legislature over data center oversight. Denver is not alone in grappling with the rapid expansion of the industry; similar moratoriums and regulatory reviews have been enacted in other U.S. cities.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature takes up ICE liability bill, housing density measures and Big Data restrictions this week
stories.summary:
The Colorado Senate is scheduled to hold its first floor vote Monday on Senate Bill 5, which would allow state residents to file civil lawsuits against federal immigration agents who injure them during enforcement operations. The bill passed its first committee on a party-line vote and is expected to clear the full Senate before moving to the House. It is the first of three immigration-related measures Democrats have prioritized this session.

Also on the legislative agenda this week are two housing density bills in the House Finance Committee. House Bill 1065 would fund ancillary transit infrastructure and affordable housing, while House Bill 1066 would provide tax incentives for land acquired for future housing development. The full House will later debate House Bill 1114, which would bar local governments from setting minimum lot sizes larger than 2,000 square feet, a measure that would allow greater residential density over local objections.

On data privacy, bipartisan bills in both chambers aim to restrict law enforcement access to personal information. Senate Bills 70 and 71 would require warrants for license plate reader databases and limit use of facial recognition and drone camera footage, respectively. A House companion measure, House Bill 1037, would prohibit private companies from selling personal data to law enforcement agencies.

Additional items include a House Judiciary debate on tenant protections, a House Ethics Committee vote on a complaint against Republican Rep. Ron Weinberg and Senate debate on sealing minors' name-change records under Senate Bill 18, which faces opposition from Gov. Jared Polis' office.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis proposes redirecting vehicle registration tax revenue to close state budget gap, alarming school districts
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis is proposing to change how Colorado counts revenue from the specific ownership tax — an annual levy tied to vehicle registrations — as a mechanism to address the state's chronic budget shortfalls. The proposal would incorporate previously "sheltered" local distributions of the tax into the state's school funding formula, effectively allowing the state to reduce its own contribution to K-12 education. In the current school year, approximately $190.5 million in specific ownership tax funds was kept by districts outside the formula.

District finance leaders say the change amounts to a funding cut. Chuck Carpenter, chief of finance for Denver Public Schools, said the proposal pulls money districts are entitled to by law. Advocacy groups and district officials contend the redistribution would harm schools that have used the funds for staffing, programs and operations, with some larger districts facing tens of millions of dollars in losses.

Polis' office argues the change would keep the state's new school funding formula solvent and distribute resources more equitably among all districts. State budget documents show total specific ownership tax distributions to all districts statewide reached an estimated $451.8 million this school year. The governor's plan would sweep the sheltered portion of those funds and redistribute them according to formula.

The proposal arrives as Colorado confronts a structural budget deficit expected to persist for multiple fiscal years. Lawmakers and education advocates are expected to clash over the plan during the current legislative session.

stories.headline: Denver DA clears four officers in fatal October gas station shooting; officer was wounded in exchange
stories.summary:
District Attorney John Walsh ruled Friday that four Denver police officers were justified in fatally shooting an armed robbery suspect at a Maverik convenience store on South Parker Road on Oct. 27, 2025. Officers Alec Garcia, Vincent Roldan, Brian Martinez and Marco Nunez shot Hosea Rashid Moore, 35, a total of 30 times after he held two female employees at gunpoint and fired on officers who entered the store. No criminal charges will be filed against the officers.

Moore entered the store at 8:41 p.m., calmly approached the checkout counter and then drew a handgun, telling a clerk to lock the doors and call police. Officers responded and attempted to enter through the front door and then a west entrance, with Roldan using a tool to shatter the glass. As the four officers moved through the store and identified themselves, Moore fired multiple shots; one struck Roldan in the upper left thigh, forcing him to apply a tourniquet on himself before being transported by ambulance to Denver Health for surgery.

After Moore fired additional rounds in the direction of officers, he was shot and killed. Walsh said the officers' "courageous actions may very well have saved lives" and that the use of force complied with Colorado law on self-defense and defense of others. Aurora police also responded to the call but were not involved in the shooting.

The ruling closes the official use-of-force review of the incident. Roldan has since recovered from his wound.

stories.headline: North Denver residents rally against CoreSite data center, demand community protections
stories.summary:
Dozens of residents from the Globeville, Elyria and Swansea neighborhoods gathered near the construction site of a CoreSite data center at East 49th Avenue and Race Street to demand the company negotiate community protections before the facility opens. The three-building campus will span more than 590,000 square feet and is CoreSite's third and largest Denver location. At full capacity, the facility could draw up to 75 megawatts of power and 805,000 gallons of water daily.

Neighborhood organizers presented CoreSite with a formal list of demands, including protections against electricity bill increases, mitigation of emissions from emergency generators, and limits on water consumption. The GES Coalition, which represents the three neighborhoods, noted that nearly all homes in the area lie within 1.5 miles of the construction site, which sits across from a park, an affordable housing building and a community health clinic.

CorrSite officials have said it is rare to operate at maximum capacity and that the first floor of the first building is expected to be complete by June. The company operates a colocation model in which multiple businesses rent server space. Full buildout of the campus is not expected until the 2030s, contingent on customer demand.

The rally occurred on the same day Mayor Mike Johnston announced his proposed moratorium on new data center construction. The Elyria-Swansea facility would not be subject to the pause because it is already under construction and has existing city permits.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC unveils inaugural 'Evergreen Kit' ahead of record-breaking NWSL home opener
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC revealed its first primary uniform Monday at Union Station, with players and hundreds of fans gathered for the launch event. The "Evergreen Kit" features a rich forest green inspired by Colorado's pine forests, with light blue and sage accents drawn from the state's sky and high desert landscapes. Center back Kaleigh Kurtz and forward Ally Brazier modeled the kit, accompanied by a Colorado Symphony performance.

Controlling owner Rob Cohen noted that the entire franchise — from the kit design to the venue — was assembled in roughly 12 months following the league's award of the expansion franchise last February. A front-of-jersey sponsor, standard in professional soccer, was not announced Monday; Cohen said a deal is forthcoming. The team's home opener is set for March 28 at Empower Field at Mile High against the Washington Spirit.

More than 45,000 tickets have already been sold for the opener, prompting the club to open additional upper-bowl seating. The current single-game NWSL attendance record is 40,091, set last year by Bay FC at Oracle Park in San Francisco. Denver Summit FC is positioned to break that mark in its first home match.

"I just knew the passion that was in this community," Cohen said. "I don't want to just break the record. I want to set a record that will stand forever."

stories.headline: Avalanche players return from Olympic gold-medal tournament; Landeskog plays first games since January injury
stories.summary:
Eight Colorado Avalanche players returned from the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan following a tournament widely praised as one of the best in hockey history. The United States defeated Canada in overtime to win gold, with Jack Hughes scoring the decisive goal. Four of the seven elimination-round games went to overtime, including Canada's quarterfinal win over the Czech Republic on a Nathan MacKinnon goal in the final minute of regulation.

Colorado's three Canadian players — MacKinnon, Cale Makar and Devon Toews — came home with silver medals, while Artturi Lehkonen and Joel Kiviranta won bronze with Finland. Brock Nelson earned gold with the U.S., becoming a third-generation Olympic gold medalist in his family. Martin Necas led Czechia with three goals and eight points before the team was eliminated in the quarterfinals.

A significant development for the Avalanche was the return of captain Gabe Landeskog, who played his first competitive games since suffering an upper-body injury Jan. 4. Landeskog had missed three full regular seasons with knee problems before returning this year. He recorded two goals and four points in five Olympic games for Sweden before the team was eliminated in the quarterfinals by the Americans.

The Avalanche, who hold the NHL's best record and lead the Western Conference, resume their regular season with a stretch of nationally televised games against Boston, Oklahoma City and Minnesota.

stories.headline: Nuggets return from All-Star break in third place in West with Peyton Watson's future looming
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets opened the second half of the season at 36-22, sitting in third place in the Western Conference with a three-game cushion over seventh-place Phoenix. Head coach David Adelman addressed the standings with his players for the first time this season upon returning from the All-Star break, emphasizing the importance of every remaining game given the conference's competitive depth. Denver faces a gauntlet of nationally televised games against Boston, Oklahoma City and Minnesota in the coming week.

Restricted free agent Peyton Watson has emerged as the central roster question for the offseason after a breakout stretch of play since January. Watson's Bird rights give Denver the ability to match any offer sheet, meaning the team could retain him without necessarily trading another player. However, the Nuggets have thus far declined to operate as a second-apron team under the current collective bargaining agreement, and Watson previously attributed the team's decision not to extend him before the season to that financial constraint.

The Nuggets' loss Sunday to a depleted Golden State Warriors team — missing Steph Curry, Jimmy Butler, Kristaps Porzingis and Draymond Green — underscored both the team's remaining vulnerabilities and the weight of each regular-season result. Denver's back-loaded home schedule could help as the team looks to improve its playoff seeding.

Aaron Gordon's recurring injury concerns have added urgency to retaining Watson as a scoring option. Denver's front office faces difficult decisions involving Watson, Cam Johnson and Christian Braun heading into the offseason.

stories.headline: Broncos head to NFL Combine in Indianapolis with linebacker, running back and tight end as top priorities
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos begin evaluating prospects at the NFL Scouting Combine in Indianapolis this week, with head coach Sean Payton and general manager George Paton seeking to address roster gaps at linebacker, running back and tight end. Denver is picking near the bottom of the first round after a 14-3 season, and an NFL scout described this year's class as one likely to yield "solid doubles" rather than top-end talent for late first-round selections.

The draft class is notable for depth at wide receiver, with 16 players ranked in Pro Football Focus's initial top-100 big board, as well as legitimate defensive line prospects in the late-first-round range. The Broncos are expected to make decisions on multiple unrestricted and restricted free agents at their priority positions before or alongside the draft.

Among the prospects drawing attention for Denver is Texas Tech inside linebacker Jacob Rodriguez at 6-foot-1 and 230 pounds. The Broncos are also expected to monitor tight end and defensive line prospects who could address depth concerns. Last year, combine evaluations led the team to draft cornerback Jahdae Barron in the first round and identify receiver Pat Bryant.

Drills begin Thursday for defensive linemen and linebackers and continue through Sunday with offensive linemen. The 2026 draft is expected to feature Indiana quarterback Fernando Mendoza as the likely No. 1 overall pick by the Las Vegas Raiders, with no other clear top-flight quarterback prospects in the class.

stories.headline: Near-record heat and gusts up to 80 mph forecast for Denver metro and Front Range this week
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service has issued a High Wind Watch for Front Range mountain and foothills counties, effective Tuesday morning through late afternoon, with sustained winds of 35 to 50 mph and gusts up to 80 mph possible. Counties under the watch include portions of Larimer, Boulder, Grand, Clear Creek, Summit and Park. The weather service warned that winds could down trees and power lines and make travel hazardous, particularly for high-profile vehicles.

In the Denver metro area, temperatures are forecast to approach or tie record highs for late February. Tuesday's projected high of 70 degrees approaches the Feb. 23 record of 71 degrees set in 2002, and Wednesday's expected 67-degree high nears the Feb. 24 record of 71 degrees from 1995. The unseasonably warm pattern is expected to persist through the workweek before a modest cooldown late in the week.

Wind-driven fire weather is the most significant concern across the eastern plains. A fire weather watch is planned for Tuesday in southeastern Colorado, including the Pueblo, Walsenburg and Trinidad areas, where wind gusts up to 30 mph and humidity values as low as 10% are expected. Forecasters warned that fires could "catch and spread quickly" and urged extreme caution with any outdoor burning.

The combination of dry conditions, warming temperatures and multiple days of gusty winds is expected to produce elevated to critical fire weather through at least Thursday across portions of the plains. The mountains may also see periods of snow showers mid-to-late week.

stories.headline: Denver lowers flags to half-staff through Saturday to honor Rev. Jesse Jackson
stories.summary:
Mayor Mike Johnston announced Sunday that flags in Denver will fly at half-staff through Saturday in memory of Rev. Jesse Jackson, the civil rights leader and two-time presidential candidate who died Feb. 18 at age 84 in Chicago. Jackson died at home surrounded by family after decades of civil rights advocacy following the assassination of Rev. Martin Luther King Jr.

Johnston called Jackson "a titan of the Civil Rights Movement" whose Rainbow Coalition "changed our nation forever." Gov. Jared Polis separately ordered flags on all state public buildings to fly at half-staff from sunrise March 6 to sunset March 7, coinciding with Jackson's scheduled public celebration of life at the 10,000-seat House of Hope church in Chicago, followed by a private memorial the next day.

Polis noted Jackson's connections to Colorado, citing relationships with former Denver Mayor Wellington Webb, his wife Wilma Webb, performer Cleo Parker Robinson and former Mayor Michael Hancock. "His contributions to Civil Rights bettered the lives of millions, inspired a generation of leaders, and moved our nation further towards justice and equality," Polis said.

Jackson, who served as a close aide to King and founded the National Rainbow Coalition, ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 1984 and 1988. He is survived by his family, who described him as "a servant leader — not only to our family, but to the oppressed, the voiceless, and the overlooked around the world."

stories.headline: Colorado Avalanche add New Mexico Goatheads as new ECHL affiliate, replacing Utah Grizzlies
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Saturday that the newly formed New Mexico Goatheads will serve as the organization's ECHL affiliate beginning with the 2026-27 season, replacing the Utah Grizzlies in that role. The Goatheads, based in Rio Rancho, will also be affiliated with the Colorado Eagles at the AHL level, creating a defined three-tier development pipeline from the ECHL through the AHL to the NHL.

The affiliation was announced at a public event at The Block arena in Rio Rancho, where the team also unveiled its mascot, a billy goat named Billy, wearing the same burgundy, steel blue and silver color scheme as the Avalanche. Fans are being invited to vote on Billy's jersey number through the team's website.

Colorado Eagles general manager and Avalanche assistant GM Kevin McDonald said the organization views the New Mexico franchise as "a crucial development pathway for players" on their way to Denver. The Avalanche currently lead the Western Conference with 37 wins, tied for the most in the NHL.

The Goatheads will play a 72-game home schedule at a Rio Rancho facility. The franchise began forming in May 2025 and follows in the footsteps of the New Mexico Scorpions, a minor league hockey team that played in the Albuquerque and Rio Rancho area from 1996 to 2009.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
